coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots with a high quantum yield and the application in labeling bovine serum albumin
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Pengpeng
Zhang, Changchang
Liu, Xiang
Cui, Ping
coredata.dc:description: An economic and green approach of manufacturing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a high quantum yield (denoted with HQY-CQDs) and the application in labeling bovine serum albumin (BSA) were described in detail in this work. Firstly, the cheap resources of citric acid and glycine were pyrolysed in drying oven for preparing the CQDs. Then the product was immersed in tetrahydrofuran for 8h. HQY-CQDs were obtained by removing tetrahydrofuran from the supernate and were evaluated that they possessed a much higher quantum yield compared with that without dealing with tetrahydrofuran and a wonderful photo-bleaching resistance. Such HQY-CQDs could be functionalized by N-hydroxysuccinimide and successively combined with BSA covalently. Thus fluorescent labeling on BSA was realized. The HQY-CQDs were demonstrated with transmission electron microscopy and the chemical modification with N-hydroxysuccinimide was proved by infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectra. Labeling BSA with the HQY-CQDs was confirmed by gel electrophoresis and fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
High fluorescence quantum yield
Functionalization with N-hydroxysuccinimide
Fluorescent labeling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CQDs with high QY
Fluorescence QY measurements of the CQDs and the HQY-CQDs
Chemical modification with NHS on the HQY-CQDs and labeling on BSA
Chemical modification on the HQY-CQDs with NHS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of manufacturing the CQDs with a high QY (denoted as HQY-CQDs) and fluorescent labeling on bovine serum albumin (BSA). Firstly, mixtures of citric acid and glycine were heated at 150°C leading to lasting dehydrations among the molecules and ultimate production of CQDs. Then the CQDs were immersed in tetrahydrofuran (THF) to extract the HQY-CQDs. The active ester was constructed via the esterification between COOH of HQY-CQDs and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) with the help of dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC). Such functionalized HQY-CQDs could bond with BSA covalently. Thus fluorescent labeling on BSA was realized.
TEM images with high-resolution of the CQDs and the HQY-CQDs corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively. Most HQY-CQDs on (b) are marked with red circles for a convenient observation.
IR spectrum of the HQY-CQDs is shown in (c). IR spectra of citric acid and glycine are also included in (a) and (b) for a clear statement about the reaction.
XP spectra of survey scan on the HQY-CQDs as well as high-resolution scans on C and N corresponding to (a) (b) and (c) respectively.

Fluorescence and UV–vis spectra of the HQY-CQDs are revealed in (a) and (b) respectively. The fluorescent photograph of the HQY-CQDs is also displayed in (a). The HQY-CQDs solution is irradiated under an ultraviolet lamp for 60min giving fluorescence spectra of (c).

Fitting line between fluorescence peak area and absorbance (<0.05) of the HQY-CQDs is exhibited in (a). Such relationship of the CQDs is also shown in (b) for announcing the enhancement of the QY with THF extraction judged from increase of the slope. The slope of the fitting line of quinine sulfate solution, as a reference, is also displayed for QY calculation of the HQY-CQDs and the CQDs.
IR spectrum and XP spectra of narrow scans on C and N of the HQY-CQDs-NHS corresponding to (a), (b) and (c) respectively.

Comparison of the gel electrophoresis images. Photographs of (a) and (b) show the SDS-PAGE gel stained with coomassie brilliant blue corresponding to the HQY-CQDs labeled and unlabeled BSA. Photographs of (c) and (d) exhibits the corresponding gel visualized by fluorescence imaging.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots with a high quantum yield and the application in labeling bovine serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An economic and green approach of manufacturing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a high quantum yield (denoted with HQY-CQDs) and the application in labeling bovine serum albumin (BSA) were described in detail in this work. Firstly, the cheap resources of citric acid and glycine were pyrolysed in drying oven for preparing the CQDs. Then the product was immersed in tetrahydrofuran for 8h. HQY-CQDs were obtained by removing tetrahydrofuran from the supernate and were evaluated that they possessed a much higher quantum yield compared with that without dealing with tetrahydrofuran and a wonderful photo-bleaching resistance. Such HQY-CQDs could be functionalized by N-hydroxysuccinimide and successively combined with BSA covalently. Thus fluorescent labeling on BSA was realized. The HQY-CQDs were demonstrated with transmission electron microscopy and the chemical modification with N-hydroxysuccinimide was proved by infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectra. Labeling BSA with the HQY-CQDs was confirmed by gel electrophoresis and fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also named as carbon nano dots or graphene quantum dots, as a new type of carbon material, have aroused extensive interests due to photoluminescence, easy manufacture, non-toxicity [1] and potential applications in photoelectric conversion [2–4]. Carbohydrates are the main raw materials for making CQDs since they are mainly composed by carbon accompanied by abundant oxygen-contained functional groups. The intramolecular elimination reactions produce plentiful vinyl groups while intermolecular dehydrations among the carbohydrates result in their jointing. Such vinyl groups construct a large π-conjugated system, which may bring the occurrence of fluorescent emission when irradiated by ultraviolet light. Citric acid was a common resource for producing CQDs depending on the three carboxyl groups and the one hydroxyl in a citric acid molecule. When heated to 200°C the solid citric acid was liquated and the color changed slowly from colorless to pale yellow [5]. That implied the dehydrations among the citric acids and production of CQDs. Although there are numerous reports about the preparations of CQDs, the descriptions about the applications of these CQDs were rare. The application of CQDs needs a higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and a proper modifying approach on CQDs. On the one hand, the CQDs with a higher QY have a more efficiency of converting absorbed light into fluorescence and emit a striking fluorescence at an extremely low concentration. That may bring an enhanced quality of fluorescent image. Therefore, CQDs with a high QY is vital for their practical applications. On the other hand, functional groups on surface of CQDs should be tuned to improve their chemical activities for special applications.

Surface passivation on CQDs is a candidate approach of improving the QY since the passivation agents repair the defects of CQDs. Some organic compounds with many functional groups can be served as the passivation agents via either physisorption or chemical bonding such as 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine [6], diamine-terminated oligomeric poly-(ethylene glycol) [7], poly(ethylene glycol) [8], linear-structured polyethyleneimine [9], etc. Peng and his coauthors reported that the passivation enhanced QY of CQDs dramatically from 0.01 to 0.13 [6]. They found that the maximum emission wavelength was almost unchanged however the half peak width decreased from 81 to 76nm. QY of the CQDs made from mixture of citric acid and poly(ethylene glycol) reached as high as 21% [8]. These exclaim that passivation may be an effective way of raising the QY of CQDs. However, such a way does not fit for developing their applications via further chemical modifications on the CQDs since the non-covalent linkages between CQDs and passivation agents can be destroyed during the modification reactions. So the applications about such passivated CQDs are restricted in some cases.

Doping heteroatom into CQDs is another proper method of improving QY of the CQDs. Carbohydrates with plentiful functional groups such as carboxyl groups tend to combine with primary amino groups of some organic compounds. For example, the CQDs derived from sole citric acid had a low QY [5]. When citric acid and ethanolamine were chosen as starting materials the QY was enhance remarkably [10]. Linear-structured polyethyleneimine with dense amino groups played the similar role in preparing N-doped CQDs [9]. The amino group combined with carboxyl group of citric acid when the mixture was subject to an intensive heating. The QY reached 37.5% at excitation wavelength of 365nm. This value was higher than that of the CQDs obtained by sole citric acid tremendously [5]. This suggested that N-doping may be a useful approach of improving the QY of CQDs. We were trying to synthesize the CQDs with a high QY and found that mixture of citric acid and glycine, as doped component, could realize the great improvement of QY compared with the CQDs made by the sole citric acid. Such cheap resources reduced the cost of manufacture greatly. Surface –COOH could also be modified facially. These will be helpful for practical applications in the future. The process is described schematically in Fig. 1 .

Citric acid monohydrate (Analytical Reagent), glycine (≥ 99.0%), NHS (99%), DCC (≥ 99.0%), quinine sulfate dihydrate (Biological Reagent), BSA (∼66kD, Biological Reagent) and THF were purchased from Xiya Reagent Company (Shandong province, China). THF was dehydrated before use. Double-distilled water was used through out the work. Phosphate buffered saline solutions (PBS, pH 7.4) were utilized as a medium of the reaction of labeling BSA with functionalized HQY-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CQDs with high QY
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolved 1.00g citric acid in 5mL water in a metallic container. Then 1.20g glycine was mixed with this solution. After they were dissolved completely, the container was placed in a drying oven for 1h at 150°C. Gradually, the added water was evaporated. Then the mixture fused and became rather thick. A large amounts of bubbles appeared from inside of the mixture. This predicted the dehydration of the mixture and the occurrence of the violent reactions under the condition of this intensive heating. The CQDs with deep yellow were obtained, described in Fig. 1. About 10mL THF were put into the container and left it out for more than 8h. The supernate was collected and the HQY-CQDs were obtained after the THF was evaporated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence QY measurements of the CQDs and the HQY-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate was chosen as a reference. QY of the CQDs and the HQY-CQDs were calculated by the following equation [11].
Y =
Y R
I
I R
A R
A
n 2
n R 2
where the subscript R refers to a reference of known QY such as quinine sulfate in this work, Y R is the QY of quinine sulfate (Y R =0.54 according to the literature [12]) which measured in 0.05M H2SO4, n is the refractive index, the value of n and n R are 1.3325 and 1.336 respectively, I and I R represents the integrated fluorescence peak areas of the sample and the reference respectively (excitation wavelength=340nm), A and A R represents the corresponding absorbance (<0.05) of ultraviolet-visible spectra of sample and reference respectively. I/A or I R/A R is the slope of the fitting line (intercept=0) between the integrated fluorescence peak area and the absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical modification with NHS on the HQY-CQDs and labeling on BSA
ce:section.ce:para: About 8mL HQY-CQDs supernates were measured and were diluted in 15mL THF (0.013gmL−1). Then 0.400g NHS and 0.110g DCC were added and disposed ultrasonically. Such esterification reaction was executed at 60°C for 3h for preparing NHS-activated HQY-CQDs (dented with HQY-CQDs-NHS). When the esterification finished the reaction vessel was placed in an ice-water bath until the excess NHS and DCC were crystallized, which were removed by a filtration. Such a disposing procedure was repeated several times until there was not any precipitate in the solution. Finally, the THF in the solution were eliminated for obtaining the HQY-CQDs-NHS.

Placed 12mL BSA (in PBS, 2.00mgmL−1) and 2mL HQY-CQDs-NHS (in PBS, 0.121gmL−1) solutions in a vessel and incubated for 8h at 25°C to realize the labeling of BSA with the HQY-CQDs. Fetched 0.5μL of such labeled and unlabeled BSA and mixed with 0.5μL loading buffers respectively. Such mixtures were input into respective tanks to employ gel electrophoresis. In the end, the SDS-PAGE gel was stained with coomassie brilliant blue and was observed in gel imaging system.

The morphologies of the CQDs and the HQY-CQDs were revealed with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company). The structural information of the HQY-CQDs and the HQY-CQDs-NHS were obtained by measurements of infrared (IR, Nicolet 380, Thermo Nicolet Corporation) and X-ray photoelectron (XP, KRATOS AMICUS) spectra. About 5μL solutions of the HQY-CQDs or the HQY-CQDs-NHS were dropped on a KBr disc, which placed under an infrared lamp for 10min to wipe out the solvent before IR measurements. XP spectra were recorded with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source. Survey scan was executed over range of 0–1350eV at steps of 1eV for elemental information. High-resolution scans on C and N were run successively to determine the atomic concentrations through the small shifts of the binding energies. The spectra were analyzed with nonlinear least-square fitting using different weighting of Gaussian–Lorentzian functions. Photoluminescence spectra (LS 45, PerkinElmer) were collected with excitation and emission slit widths of 15nm. Ultraviolet-visible spectra (specord 200, Analytic Jena AG) were recorded and corrected by subtracting the background signals of the solvent. The fluorescent labeling on BSA was judged by the photograph of gel imaging (UVP GelMax Imager System, USA).

Many approaches of preparing CQDs derived from citric acid [5,8–10,13–15] had been reported, which demonstrated that citric acid was an ideal material for manufacturing CQDs. On account of the comparatively lower melting point citric acids are melted entirely when heated to 150°C in a drying oven. The melted citric acids facilitate the dehydration among the molecules, which leads to the jointing of the molecules via loss of water until the production of the CQDs finally. Differed with previous reports [5,8,15], we dissolve the raw materials with several milliliters’ water in the reaction vessel for synthesizing the CQDs. At the initial stage the reaction, the water is evaporated and thus the reactants are mixed quite uniformly, which should bring about the homogeneous reactions between the two components. Pyrolysis causes breaking down of carboxyl group accompanied by production of methyl. Such an electron-donating group will influence the fluorescent emission. Elimination of hydroxyl results in vinyl, which contributes for the formations of conjugated or co-plane species. Judged from the chemical essences, glycine with primary amino and carboxyl groups can participate in such processes and therefore N elements can be doped in the CQDs. As we all know that glycine has a high melting point, the sole glycine cannot be used in preparing CQDs via pyrolysis in this case. However, when glycines are covered by the melted citric acids they are involved in the dehydration reaction quickly for we can not observe the white crystals of glycine at the end of the reaction. Such nitrogen atoms are included in the conjugated system due to the p–π conjugation, which may effects on the photoluminescence of the CQDs.

Morphology of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2(a). It is obvious that the CQDs are aggregate, which should be attributable to the excess citric acid or glycine. Such unconverted salts bring down the stability of the CQDs in aqueous media and resultantly induce the aggregation like the stability of colloid will be depressed by added inorganic salts. The excess reactants can be removed after the CQDs are extracted with THF for citric acid or glycine is completely insoluble in THF. Such an approach helps us purify the prepared CQDs. Although dialysis tube could be applied in purification of CQDs as described in many reports [15,16] we found that some CQDs could travel though the membrane. Fig. 2(b) reveals the HQY-CQDs disperse homogeneously after removal of the unconverted reactants and high crystallinity of the pure CQDs with a lattice-spacing distance of approximate 0.280nm. Most important of all, such an extraction operation improves the QY extremely, which will be stated below.

IR spectra of Fig. 3 tell us that structure of the CQDs differs obviously with citric acid and glycine. In Fig. 3(a), bands of 3382 and 1729cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of OH (v(OH)) and C=O (v(CO)) [5] respectively. The information of primary amino and carboxyl are ambiguous in Fig. 3(b) since the amino and carboxyl groups form an intramolecular salt (+NH3CH2COO−). Stretching vibration of NH2 (v(NH)) becomes unclear while the v(CO) of COOH disappears completely in Fig. 3(b). Bands of 1616 and 1512cm–1 should be due to asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of carboxylate ions (OCO−). In Fig. 3(c), band of v(CO) differs with that of citric acid and shifts from 1729 to 1712cm−1. Peak centered at 3443cm−1 should be the overlapping of v(OH) and v(NH). Band of 1183cm−1 is attributable to the vibration of COC, which predicts the intermolecular dehydration while 1631cm−1 is due to the v(CC) that reveals intramolecular dehydration. The IR spectrum also witnesses the existence of methyl and methylene of the HQY-CQDs, marked with a gray rectangle. All these demonstrate there are remarkable alterations when citric acid and glycine are suffered from such an intensive heating.

The chemical composites of the HQY-CQDs can also be disclosed by XP spectrum, which shown in Fig. 4 . Survey scan tells us the elemental information, which exclaims that C, O and N are the main ingredients in the HQY-CQDs displayed in Fig. 4(a). The C 1s core-level spectrum of Fig. 4(b) can be curve-fitted with four peak components at about 284.7, 285.3, 286.5 and 288.8eV respectively. The binding energy of 284.7 should be attributable to the C C, C C or C H [14] while the value of 285.3eV should belong to C N [14]. The value of 286.5 and 288.8eV should be assigned to C O and O C O respectively [17]. Based on the analysis of the peak areas of Fig. 4(b), we conclude that the C C, C C or C H are the main components, which construct the skeleton of the HQY-CQDs. Intramolecular dehydration creates large amounts of CC, which contributes for the rigid skeletons of the HQY-CQDs. The CO components should come from carbon baring hydroxyl or COC. This suggests that the intermolecular dehydration results in numerous linkages of COC, which devotes the jointing of the reactants. Besides, peak areas of O C–O are quite remarkable in Fig. 4(b), which announces their high proportions and the large amount of carboxyl groups in the HQY-CQDs. The components of CN demonstrate that glycine participates in the production of the HYQ-CQDs and nitrogen elements are doped in the HQY-CQDs, which is also witnessed by Fig. 4(c).

Fig. 4(c) distinctly declares the doped N in the HQY-CQDs. Binding energy of 400.3eV, attributable to N C or N H [17,18], discloses the amino groups in the HQY-CQDs. Such amino groups are prone to be protonated by ionized protons of carboxyl groups. Hence binding energy of 401.7eV should be due to the NH3 + [19,20]. The enhancement of QY, resulted from mixed glycine, will be evaluated by a comparison with quinine sulfate in 0.05M sulfuric acid. Apparently, this approach of doping N elements in CQDs is extremely convenient. That should be due to the chemical activities of the amino and carboxyl groups of glycine.

The photoluminescence and UV–vis spectra are exhibited in Fig. 5(a) and (b) respectively. The emission wavelengths, shown in Fig. 5(a), remain unchanged when the HQY-CQDs are irradiated by lights with varied wavelength ranged from 290 to 340nm. This predicts the homogeneous particle sizes and uniform structures of all the HYQ-CQDs. At the initial stage of the reaction, the mixed solutions of citric acid and glycine are homogeneous completely in the reaction vessel. This ensures the structural homogeneity of the products. That is the reason why we choose aqueous solutions of citric acid and glycine as starting materials rather than the solid ones. UV–vis spectrum of Fig. 5(b) shows that there are two absorption peaks at 250 and 340nm. The photograph of photoluminescence is also shown in Fig. 5(a). The HYQ-CQDs solutions (1.54×10−4 gmL−1) emit intensive blue fluorescence when irradiated by a portable ultraviolet lamp. Judged from the brightness and the fluorescence intensity of the HYQ-CQDs solution with such a low concentration we speculate that such CQDs are supposed to have a high fluorescence QY. The photo-bleaching resistance of the HQY-CQDs is demonstrated by Fig. 5(c). The fluorescence intensities of the HYQ-CQDs solution are almost the same when the solution is irradiated by an ultraviolet lamp for 60min. Such a performance declares a better photo-bleaching resistance of the HYQ-CQDs compared with the fluorescein in Cy5 [21].

Fluorescence QY was measured by the comparison with quinine sulfate in 0.05M sulfuric acid. The absorbances of the measured solutions are controlled within 0.05. Linear fitting is applied in the relationship between the fluorescence peak area and absorbance for obtaining the slope of the line, which shown in Fig. 6 . The QY of the HYQ-CQDs and the CQDs are 59.8% and 20.8% respectively. Therefore, the extraction with THF enhances the QY extremely. This may be derived from removal of the excess reactants which increase the absorbance in QY measurements and larger solubility of the HQY-CQDs in THF. To the best of my knowledge, this is much greater than most of the reported CQDs [8–10,14]. Such a facile extraction operation can not only eliminate the impurities but also enhance the QY remarkably. This is more convenient than the separation of CQDs with dialysis tube. The high QY means the high photoluminescence efficiency which will benefit for applications of the HYQ-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical modification on the HQY-CQDs with NHS
ce:section.ce:para: Although the N-contained passivation agents did raise the QY of CQDs [9], the combination between them depended on hydrogen bond or Van der Waals force. Such a weak interaction cannot undergo further chemical modifications since the structure was easy to be destroyed. For the HQY-CQDs, the doped components are combined covalently and the structure is stable enough to suffer from further chemical tunings. Aqueous solution of the HQY-CQDs shows acidic at pH=3, which ascribed to their carboxyl groups. This is proved by IR spectrum of Fig. 3 and XP spectrum of Fig. 4(b). Carboxyl groups can be activated by NHS giving active NHS ester, which is reactive for the primary amino of biomolecules [17,22]. Thus it is much likely that the HQY-CQDs can be modified by NHS giving NHS-activated HQY-CQDs (denoted with HQY-CQDs-NHS), which demonstrated by IR and XP spectra of Fig. 7 .

In Fig. 7(a), band of 1780cm−1 should be assigned to the coupling of symmetrical stretching vibration of NHS moiety (OCNCO) while 1815cm−1 is due to the v(CO) in the resulted ester [23,24]. That means the transformation from carboxyl into NHS ester. C 1s core-level spectrum is spitted into five peaks which represent the five different components noted in Fig. 7(b). Binding energy of 288.9eV is still due to the O C O of unconverted carboxyl or resulted ester. The ratio of [CC]/[O C O] increase from 6.2, calculated from Fig. 4(b), to 8.2 from Fig. 7(b), which should be arisen from the introduced NHS moieties. Binding energy of 287.0eV is attributed to new occurred component of OCN, which also come from NHS species. All these declare the completion of the esterification. However, not all the carboxyl groups are changed into NHS esters. Some COOH combine with NH2 generating +NH3 COO−, which demonstrated by N 1s core-level spectrum of Fig. 7(c). Binding energy of 401.7eV is still due to the NH3 + component. The newly occurred component of NO (402.6eV) is also derived from the NHS fragments, which confirm the esterification reaction.

It is well known to all that Cy3 or Cy5 [25] is commonly used in fluorescent labeling of biomolecules such as polypeptides or proteins. In Cy3 or Cy5, the fluorescein is modified chemically by a NHS ester which performs reactive with biomolecules baring primary amino group. Nevertheless, the fluorescent moieties are liable to be destroyed when irradiated by ultraviolet lights [21], which is unfavorable for researches to observe the fluorescent image for a long time. Apart from this, such NHS-functionalized fluoresceins are quite expensive because of the complicated syntheses [21]. The HQY-CQDs with a high QY and the facile modification by the chemically bonded NHS esters will promote their applications such as labeling of biomolecules. Herein, we choose BSA as an example of biomolecule to demonstrate the activity of the HQY-CQDs-NHS. The experimental results of gel electrophoresis are revealed in Fig. 8 .

In Fig. 8, strip (a) shows the slight increase of molecular weigh of the labeled BSA compared with unlabeled BSA shown in (b). Such a slight increase is attributed to smaller molecular weigh of the HQY-CQDs compared with the BSA (∼66kD). However, strip (c) clearly exhibits that BSA is labeled by the fluorescent HQY-CQDs while the unlabeled BSA has not such performances shown in (d). What is more, the HQY-CQDs are bonded with BSA covalently indeed. They are not separated from the labeled BSA during the gel electrophoresis. All these prove the success of labeling BSA with the HQY-CQDs.

We have designed a strategy of preparing HQY-CQDs with a high QY. Such cheap raw materials and the facile way of synthesizing HQY-CQDs-NHS bring down the cost of preparing fluorescent labels. The chemically bonded NHS esters show wonderful activity to the primary amino groups of BSA. It is believed that more biomolecules with primary amino groups can be labeled by the HQY-CQDs-NHS. As a new biological probe, the HQY-CQDs-NHS has extensively potential applications in fluorescent labeling of biomolecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (RD15100025) and the helpful discussions with Dr. Liang Ma, Zi Liu and Lei Bi.
